**Normandy**[[note 2]](#cite_note-4) is a geographical and cultural region in northwestern Europe, roughly coextensive with the historical [Duchy of Normandy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Normandy "Duchy of Normandy").

Normandy comprises [mainland Normandy](/wiki/Normandy_(administrative_region) "Normandy (administrative region)") (a part of France) and insular Normandy (mostly the British [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands")). It covers 30,627 square kilometres (11,825 sq mi).[[3]](#cite_note-5) Its population in 2017 was 3,499,280. The inhabitants of Normandy are known as Normans;[[1]](#cite_note-Denonym-2) the region is the historic homeland of the [Norman language](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language"). Large settlements include [Rouen](/wiki/Rouen "Rouen"), [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen"), [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre") and [Cherbourg](/wiki/Cherbourg-en-Cotentin "Cherbourg-en-Cotentin").

The cultural region of Normandy is roughly similar to the historical Duchy of Normandy, which includes small areas now part of the departments of [Mayenne](/wiki/Mayenne "Mayenne") and [Sarthe](/wiki/Sarthe "Sarthe"). The Channel Islands (French: *Îles Anglo-Normandes*) are also historically part of Normandy; they cover 194 square kilometres (75 sq mi)[[4]](#cite_note-6) and comprise two [bailiwicks](/wiki/Bailiwick "Bailiwick"): [Guernsey](/wiki/Bailiwick_of_Guernsey "Bailiwick of Guernsey") and [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey"), which are British Crown Dependencies.

Normandy's name comes from the settlement of the territory by [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") ("[Northmen](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen")") starting in the 9th century, and confirmed by treaty in the 10th century between King [Charles III of France](/wiki/Charles_the_Simple "Charles the Simple") and the Viking *[jarl](/wiki/Earl "Earl")* [Rollo](/wiki/Rollo "Rollo"). For almost 150 years following the [Norman conquest of England](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England") in 1066, Normandy and England were linked by having the same person reign as both [Duke of Normandy](/wiki/Duke_of_Normandy "Duke of Normandy") and [King of England](/wiki/King_of_England "King of England").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Normandy](/wiki/History_of_Normandy "History of Normandy")

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

Archaeological finds, such as [cave paintings](/wiki/Cave_paintings "Cave paintings"), prove that humans were present in the region in prehistoric times. Normandy also has many [megalithic monuments](/wiki/Megalithic_monument "Megalithic monument").[[5]](#cite_note-7)
Human presence in the region does not date back earlier than the end of the [Lower Paleolithic](/wiki/Lower_Paleolithic "Lower Paleolithic") (before that, this region was extremely cold). In the [Middle Paleolithic](/wiki/Middle_Paleolithic "Middle Paleolithic"), it is attested by numerous finds of [lithic industry](/w/index.php?title=Stone_tool_industry&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stone tool industry (page does not exist)"). But, in the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic"), the region was occupied by [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra"), which was not very favorable to human life. However, it would be inhabited again, as shown by the [Gouy cave](/w/index.php?title=Gouy_cave&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gouy cave (page does not exist)") near [Rouen](/wiki/Rouen "Rouen"), which, due to its [parietal engravings](/wiki/Parietal_art "Parietal art") dated to the [Magdalenian](/wiki/Magdalenian "Magdalenian"), happens to be the northernmost decorated cave in Europe. In addition, many still-visible [megaliths](/wiki/Megaliths "Megaliths") are scattered quite regularly throughout the Norman countryside.[[6]](#cite_note-8) The [Rozel archaeological site](/w/index.php?title=Rozel_archaeological_site&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rozel archaeological site (page does not exist)") presents exceptional traces of footprints and handprints of *[Homo neanderthalensis](/wiki/Homo_neanderthalensis "Homo neanderthalensis")*.

But it is truly only in the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") (between 2300 and 800 BC) that Normandy began to be developed. At this time, farms, field systems, and vast necropolises were established in the territory, forming an initial network of sites covering the whole of Norman land.[[7]](#cite_note-bookMarc-9)

The discovery of objects such as the [golden Gallic helmet of Amfreville-sous-les-Monts](/w/index.php?title=Amfreville_Helmet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amfreville Helmet (page does not exist)") or the iron one from the [Louviers museum](/w/index.php?title=Louviers_museum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Louviers museum (page does not exist)"), as well as sites such as the large [Pîtres necropolis](/w/index.php?title=P%C3%AEtres_necropolis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pîtres necropolis (page does not exist)")[[8]](#cite_note-10) ([Eure](/wiki/Eure_(department) "Eure (department)")), with its cremation urns, coiled swords and traces of [chariot tombs](/wiki/Chariot_burial "Chariot burial"), or the [necropolis](/wiki/Necropolis "Necropolis") of [Ifs](/wiki/Ifs,_Calvados "Ifs, Calvados") ([Calvados](/wiki/Calvados_(department) "Calvados (department)")), dating from the end of the [Hallstatt period](/wiki/Hallstatt_culture "Hallstatt culture") or the beginning of the [La Tène](/wiki/La_T%C3%A8ne_culture "La Tène culture") period, testify to Celtic presence in Normandy. The [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") peoples of present-day Normandy were part of [Armorica](/wiki/Armorica "Armorica"), a confederation of culturally close peoples along the shores of the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), from the [estuary of the Seine](/w/index.php?title=Seine_estuary&action=edit&redlink=1 "Seine estuary (page does not exist)") to that of the [Loire](/w/index.php?title=Loire_estuary&action=edit&redlink=1 "Loire estuary (page does not exist)").

The testimony of [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar") (in the [*Gallic Wars*](/wiki/Commentarii_de_Bello_Gallico "Commentarii de Bello Gallico")) allows us to identify the different [Gallic](/wiki/Gauls "Gauls") groups occupying the region. In 56 or 57 BC, these populations gathered to resist the invasion of the [Roman legions](/wiki/Roman_legion "Roman legion"). After the Gallic defeat at the [siege of Alesia](/wiki/Battle_of_Alesia "Battle of Alesia"), the peoples of Normandy continued the struggle for some time, but by 51 BC, all of Gaul was subdued by Rome.[[7]](#cite_note-bookMarc-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Mosa%C3%AEc_Lillebonne_Orph%C3%A9e.jpg/250px-Mosa%C3%AEc_Lillebonne_Orph%C3%A9e.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mosa%C3%AEc_Lillebonne_Orph%C3%A9e.jpg)

[Great Lillebonne mosaic](/wiki/Great_Lillebonne_mosaic "Great Lillebonne mosaic"), in [Seine-Maritime](/wiki/Seine-Maritime "Seine-Maritime"), kept in the [departmental Museum of Antiquities in Rouen](/wiki/Museum_of_Antiquities_(Rouen) "Museum of Antiquities (Rouen)").

Between 27 BC and 15 BC,[[9]](#cite_note-11) Emperor [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") reorganized the Gallic territory and moved the [Caletes](/wiki/Caletes "Caletes") and the [Veliocasses](/wiki/Veliocasses "Veliocasses") into the province of [Gallia Lugdunensis](/wiki/Gallia_Lugdunensis "Gallia Lugdunensis"), whose capital was [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"). The Romanization of Normandy, as elsewhere in the West, involved the construction of roads and towns.

Many [Gallo-Roman villas](/wiki/Roman_villa "Roman villa") are known in Norman territory. Builders used local materials: [flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint"), [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk"), [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [brick](/wiki/Brick "Brick"), [wattle and daub](/wiki/Wattle_and_daub "Wattle and daub"). Heating of baths or certain rooms used the Roman [hypocaust](/wiki/Hypocaust "Hypocaust") system (suburban villa at [Vieux-la-Romaine](/w/index.php?title=Vieux-la-Romaine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vieux-la-Romaine (page does not exist)")).[[10]](#cite_note-12)

Agriculture provided [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") and [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax"), according to [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder"). Finally, in the Norman countryside of [Antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"), the [*fana*](/wiki/Glossary_of_ancient_Roman_religion#fanum "Glossary of ancient Roman religion") (small square-centered temples of Celtic tradition) were numerous. One example is located west of [Harfleur](/wiki/Harfleur "Harfleur"). Excavations have also revealed many terracotta statuettes of mother goddesses in Norman tombs and houses. Thus, at [Vieil-Évreux](/wiki/Gisacum "Gisacum"), there existed one of the most important pilgrimage centers in Europe, which included a [forum](/wiki/Forum_(Roman) "Forum (Roman)"), [Roman baths](/wiki/Roman_baths "Roman baths"), a monumental [basilica](/w/index.php?title=Basilica_(Roman)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Basilica (Roman) (page does not exist)"), two *fana* and the second largest [theater](/wiki/Roman_theatre_(structure) "Roman theatre (structure)") in [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul").[[11]](#cite_note-13)

From the second third of the 3rd century, "barbarian" raids devastated many places in the Norman region. The coastline had to face maritime piracy from the [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons"), but also from the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") and [Frisians](/wiki/Frisians "Frisians"). Germanic contingents were therefore recruited by the Roman army to fight against other Germans,[[note 3]](#cite_note-14) and these immigrants were granted permission to settle in the Empire.[[12]](#cite_note-bookRed-15)

During the reforms of Emperor [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") (285–305), the future Normandy became distinct by forming the [Lyonnaise Secunda](/wiki/Gallia_Lugdunensis "Gallia Lugdunensis"), whose borders foreshadowed those of ducal Normandy seven centuries later: it stretched from the [Couesnon](/wiki/Couesnon "Couesnon") to the [Bresle](/wiki/Bresle "Bresle") and was bounded to the south by the upper courses of the [Sarthe](/wiki/Sarthe_(river) "Sarthe (river)") and the [Avre](/w/index.php?title=Avre_(Eure_tributary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Avre (Eure tributary) (page does not exist)"). The only significant difference was that Lyonnaise Secunda included the future [Vexin français](/wiki/Vexin_fran%C3%A7ais "Vexin français"), with the land of the [Veliocasses](/wiki/Veliocasses "Veliocasses") then remaining undivided, and these immigrants were granted permission to settle in the Empire.[[12]](#cite_note-bookRed-15)

### Celtic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Celtic period")]

[Celts](/wiki/Celts "Celts") (also known as [Belgae](/wiki/Belgae "Belgae") and [Gauls](/wiki/Gauls "Gauls")) have populated Normandy since at least the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"). When [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar") invaded Gaul (58–50 BC), there were nine different Celtic tribes living in this part of Gaul.[[13]](#cite_note-16)

### Romanisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Romanisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/TheatreLillebonne.jpg/250px-TheatreLillebonne.jpg)](/wiki/File:TheatreLillebonne.jpg)

[Gallo-Roman theatre](/wiki/Roman_theatre_(structure) "Roman theatre (structure)") in [Lillebonne](/wiki/Lillebonne "Lillebonne")

The [Romanisation](/wiki/Romanization_(cultural) "Romanization (cultural)") of this region partly included in the *[Gallia Celtica](/wiki/Gallia_Celtica "Gallia Celtica")* and in the *[Gallia Belgica](/wiki/Gallia_Belgica "Gallia Belgica")* (the Seine being more or less the limit between them) was achieved by the usual methods: [Roman roads](/wiki/Roman_roads "Roman roads") and a policy of urbanisation. [Classicists](/wiki/Classicist "Classicist") mention many [Gallo-Roman](/wiki/Gallo-Roman "Gallo-Roman") [villas](/wiki/Villa "Villa") and archeology found their traces in the past 30 years. In the [Late Roman Empire](/wiki/Late_Roman_Empire "Late Roman Empire") a new province was created and called *[Lugdunensis Secunda](/wiki/Gallia_Lugdunensis "Gallia Lugdunensis")*, it sketched the later [ecclesiastical province of Rouen](/wiki/Archbishopric_of_Rouen "Archbishopric of Rouen"), with the *Metropolis civitas Rotomagensium*
([Rouen](/wiki/Rouen "Rouen")), *Civitas Baiocassium* (*Augustodorum*, Bayeux), *Civitas Abrincatum* (*Ingena*, Avranches), *Civitas Ebroicorum* (*Mediolanum*, Évreux), *Civitas Saiorum* (Sées), *Civitas Lexoviorum* (*Noviomagus*, Lisieux / Lieuvin) and *Civitas Constantia* (Coutances).[[14]](#cite_note-17)

### Germanic invasions and settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Germanic invasions and settlements")]

In the late 3rd century AD, Germanic raids devastated Lugdunensis Secunda, as the modern area of Normandy was known at the time. The Romans built a system of coastal defences known as [Saxon Shore](/wiki/Saxon_Shore "Saxon Shore") on both sides of the English Channel. Coastal settlements were raided by [Saxon](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons") pirates that finally settled mainly in the [Bessin](/wiki/Bessin "Bessin") region.[[15]](#cite_note-18) Modern archeology reveals their presence in different Merovingian cemeteries excavated east of Caen.[[16]](#cite_note-19) Christianity also began to enter the area during this period and Rouen already had a metropolitan bishop by the 4th century. The ecclesiastical province of Rouen was based on the frame of the Roman *Lugdunensis Secunda*, whose limits corresponded almost exactly to the future [duchy](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") of Normandy. In 406, [Germanic tribes](/wiki/List_of_early_Germanic_peoples "List of early Germanic peoples") began invading from the east, followed by dispersed settlements mainly in the [Pays de Bray](/wiki/Pays_de_Bray "Pays de Bray"), [Pays de Caux](/wiki/Pays_de_Caux "Pays de Caux") and [Vexin](/wiki/Vexin "Vexin"). As early as 487, the area between the rivers [Somme](/wiki/Somme_(river) "Somme (river)") and [Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire") came under the control of the [Frankish](/wiki/Franks "Franks") lord [Clovis](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I").

## The Franks and the Scandinavian invasions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The Franks and the Scandinavian invasions")]

### Frankish rule after Rome

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Frankish rule after Rome")]

Following the disintegration of Roman power in northern Gaul, the region that would later become Normandy passed under the control of the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks"). By the sixth and seventh centuries it was integrated into the [Merovingian](/wiki/Merovingian_dynasty "Merovingian dynasty") and later [Carolingian](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire") realms. Large rural estates, episcopal sees such as Rouen, and fiscal centres marked Frankish authority. The [Capitulary](/wiki/Capitulary "Capitulary") legislation and royal courts extended Frankish law and institutions into the area, though local aristocracies maintained significant autonomy.[[17]](#cite_note-20)

By the ninth century, Carolingian kings such as [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") and [Louis the Pious](/wiki/Louis_the_Pious "Louis the Pious") maintained river defenses and fleets, but the weakening of central power after 840 created vulnerabilities. Political divisions between the heirs of Louis the Pious, as codified in the [Treaty of Verdun](/wiki/Treaty_of_Verdun "Treaty of Verdun") (843), fractured the Frankish kingdom and left the Seine valley especially exposed to external attack.[[18]](#cite_note-21)

### Viking raids on the Seine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Viking raids on the Seine")]

From the late eighth century, Scandinavian raiders targeted the coasts of northern Gaul. Monastic centres such as [Jumièges Abbey](/wiki/Jumi%C3%A8ges_Abbey "Jumièges Abbey") and [Saint-Wandrille](/wiki/Saint-Wandrille "Saint-Wandrille") were plundered repeatedly; Rouen itself was attacked and temporarily abandoned in 841.[[19]](#cite_note-22) Viking fleets exploited the navigability of the Seine and its tributaries, sailing upriver to raid Paris in 845 under a leader recorded as Ragnar. Annals such as the *[Annales Bertiniani](/wiki/Annales_Bertiniani "Annales Bertiniani")* describe the devastation of monasteries and towns and the payment of tribute by Frankish rulers to secure temporary peace.[[20]](#cite_note-23)

Over time, groups of Scandinavians began to overwinter in the lower Seine valley, constructing fortified encampments and establishing trading outposts. Archaeological finds of weapon deposits, silver hoards and Scandinavian-style burials indicate semi-permanent settlement before 911.[[21]](#cite_note-24)

### Viking raids and foundation of the Norman state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Viking raids and foundation of the Norman state")]

[Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") started to raid along the river Seine during the middle of the 9th century. As early as 841, a Viking fleet appeared at the mouth of the Seine, the principal route by which they entered the kingdom.[[22]](#cite_note-25) After attacking and destroying monasteries, including one at [Jumièges](/wiki/Jumi%C3%A8ges "Jumièges"), they took advantage of the power vacuum created by the disintegration of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne")'s empire to take Northern France. The [fiefdom of Normandy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Normandy "Duchy of Normandy") was created for the Viking leader *Hrólfr*, known in [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") as *Rollo*. Rollo had [besieged Paris](/wiki/Siege_of_Paris_(885%E2%80%93886) "Siege of Paris (885–886)") but in 911 entered [vassalage](/wiki/Vassal "Vassal") to the king of the [West Franks](/wiki/Western_Francia "Western Francia"), [Charles the Simple](/wiki/Charles_the_Simple "Charles the Simple"), through the [Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Clair-sur-Epte "Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte"). In exchange for his [homage](/wiki/Homage_(feudal) "Homage (feudal)") and [fealty](/wiki/Fealty "Fealty"), Rollo legally gained the territory that he and his Viking allies had previously conquered. The name "Normandy" reflects Rollo's Viking ("[Norseman](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen")") origins.

### The Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte (911)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte (911)")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section may **require [cleanup](/wiki/Wikipedia:Cleanup "Wikipedia:Cleanup")** to meet Wikipedia's [quality standards](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style "Wikipedia:Manual of Style"). The specific problem is: **misused asterisks for non-English words.** Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Normandy "Special:EditPage/Normandy") if you can. *(October 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The turning point came in 911, when a major Viking host under the leadership of [Rollo](/wiki/Rollo "Rollo") (Old Norse "Hrólfr", Latin "Rollo" or "Rolf") clashed with West Frankish forces near Chartres. Following negotiations, King [Charles the Simple](/wiki/Charles_the_Simple "Charles the Simple") and Rollo concluded an agreement, known as the [Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Clair-sur-Epte "Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte"). According to later tradition, the treaty granted Rollo and his followers land around the lower Seine, centered on Rouen, in return for conversion to Christianity, homage to the king, and an obligation to defend the coast against further Viking attacks.[[23]](#cite_note-26)[[24]](#cite_note-27)

While the exact extent of the original grant is debated, it likely encompassed the Rouen region and surrounding territory. Over subsequent decades, Rollo's successors expanded this domain westward to include the Cotentin and eastward into the Pays de Caux, laying the foundations of the [Duchy of Normandy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Normandy "Duchy of Normandy").[[25]](#cite_note-28)

The descendants of *Rollo* and his followers created an aristocracy that step by step adopted the local [Gallo-Romance language](/wiki/Gallo-Romance_languages "Gallo-Romance languages"), intermarried with the area's native Gallo-Frankish inhabitants, and adopted Christianity. Nevertheless, the first generations of Scandinavian and Anglo-Scandinavian settlers brought slaves, mainly from the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), and often turned the women into *[frilla](/wiki/Concubine "Concubine")*, a Scandinavian tradition which became known as *[more Danico](/wiki/More_danico "More danico")*, medieval Latin meaning "Danish marriage". The first counts of Rouen and the dukes of Normandy had concubines too. While very little archeological excavations about the Vikings were done in Normandy, the [Norman toponymy](/wiki/Norman_toponymy "Norman toponymy") retains a large Scandinavian and Anglo-Scandinavian heritage, due to a constant use of [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") during four or five generations in certain parts of Normandy.

They then became the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") – a [Norman French](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language")-speaking mixture of Norsemen and indigenous Gallo-Franks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Bayeux_Tapestry_scene23_Harold_sacramentum_fecit_Willelmo_duci.jpg/250px-Bayeux_Tapestry_scene23_Harold_sacramentum_fecit_Willelmo_duci.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bayeux_Tapestry_scene23_Harold_sacramentum_fecit_Willelmo_duci.jpg)

[Bayeux Tapestry](/wiki/Bayeux_Tapestry "Bayeux Tapestry") (Scene 23): [Harold II](/wiki/Harold_Godwinson "Harold Godwinson") swearing oath on holy relics to [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror")

Rollo's descendant [William](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") became king of England in 1066 after defeating [Harold Godwinson](/wiki/Harold_Godwinson "Harold Godwinson"), the last of the [Anglo-Saxon kings](/wiki/History_of_Anglo-Saxon_England "History of Anglo-Saxon England"), at the [Battle of Hastings](/wiki/Battle_of_Hastings "Battle of Hastings"), while retaining the [fiefdom](/wiki/Fiefdom "Fiefdom") of Normandy for himself and his descendants.

### Norman chroniclers and historical memory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Norman chroniclers and historical memory")]

The earliest extended narrative of the Norman foundation is the "Historia Normannorum" of [Dudo of Saint-Quentin](/wiki/Dudo_of_Saint-Quentin "Dudo of Saint-Quentin"), composed around 1015–1030 for Duke Richard I and Richard II. Dudo presents Rollo as a pious founder and constructs a genealogy linking the dukes with both Frankish royalty and heroic Scandinavian origins. Though literary and partisan, Dudo's account shaped later chronicles such as those of William of Jumièges and Orderic Vitalis, and influenced medieval and modern views of the Norman beginnings.[[26]](#cite_note-29)[[27]](#cite_note-30)

### Norman expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Norman expansion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Norman_Conquests_copy_%281%29.jpg/250px-Norman_Conquests_copy_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norman_Conquests_copy_(1).jpg)

A chronological map of the Norman Conquests, including England (1066–1485, not always in personal union with Normandy), Normandy (911–1204), [southern Italy and Sicily](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_southern_Italy "Norman conquest of southern Italy") (1030–1263), [parts of Africa around Tripoli](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Africa "Kingdom of Africa") (1146–1158), and the [Crusader state](/wiki/Crusader_state "Crusader state") of the [Principality of Antioch](/wiki/Principality_of_Antioch "Principality of Antioch") along with associated vassals, the Principality of Ancyra (1073–1075), the [Principality of Tarragona](/wiki/Principality_of_Tarragona "Principality of Tarragona") (1129–1173), and the [Kingdom of the Canary Islands](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Canary_Islands "Kingdom of the Canary Islands")

Aside from the conquest of England and the subsequent invasions of Wales and Ireland, the Normans expanded into other areas. Norman families, such as that of [Tancred of Hauteville](/wiki/Tancred_of_Hauteville "Tancred of Hauteville"), [Rainulf Drengot](/wiki/Rainulf_Drengot "Rainulf Drengot") and [Guimond de Moulins](/wiki/Guimond_de_Moulins "Guimond de Moulins") played important parts in the [conquest of southern Italy](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_southern_Italy "Norman conquest of southern Italy") and the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades").

The [Drengot](/wiki/Drengot_family "Drengot family") lineage, de Hauteville's sons [William Iron Arm](/wiki/William_Iron_Arm "William Iron Arm"), [Drogo](/wiki/Drogo_of_Hauteville "Drogo of Hauteville"), and [Humphrey](/wiki/Humphrey_of_Hauteville "Humphrey of Hauteville"), [Robert Guiscard](/wiki/Robert_Guiscard "Robert Guiscard") and [Roger the Great Count](/wiki/Roger_I_of_Sicily "Roger I of Sicily") progressively claimed territories in southern Italy until founding the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily") in 1130. They also carved out a place for themselves and their descendants in the [Crusader states](/wiki/Crusader_states "Crusader states") of [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor") and the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land").

The 14th-century explorer [Jean de Béthencourt](/wiki/Jean_de_B%C3%A9thencourt "Jean de Béthencourt") established a [kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Canary_Islands "Kingdom of the Canary Islands") in the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands") in 1404. He received the title King of the Canary Islands from Pope [Innocent VII](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_VII "Pope Innocent VII") but recognized [Henry III of Castile](/wiki/Henry_III_of_Castile "Henry III of Castile") as his overlord, who had provided him with military and financial aid during the conquest.

### Integration and the rise of the Norman duchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Integration and the rise of the Norman duchy")]

Over the tenth century the Scandinavian newcomers gradually merged with the Frankish population. Rollo's baptism and the establishment of a Norman episcopate symbolized Christianization, while intermarriage and bilingualism facilitated cultural assimilation. By the mid-eleventh century the dukes of Normandy commanded a polity that blended Scandinavian martial traditions with Frankish legal, ecclesiastical and feudal practices.[[28]](#cite_note-31)

### 13th to 17th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 13th to 17th centuries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Joan_of_arc_burning_at_stake.jpg/250px-Joan_of_arc_burning_at_stake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joan_of_arc_burning_at_stake.jpg)

[Joan of Arc](/wiki/Joan_of_Arc "Joan of Arc") about to be burned at the stake in the city of [Rouen](/wiki/Rouen "Rouen"), painting by [Jules Eugène Lenepveu](/wiki/Jules_Eug%C3%A8ne_Lenepveu "Jules Eugène Lenepveu")

In 1204, during the reign of [John, King of England](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England"), mainland Normandy was captured from the English by the forces of [Philip II of France](/wiki/Philip_II_of_France "Philip II of France"), ending some 293 years of relative Norman independence from the French crown. Insular Normandy (the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands")) remained under control of the English, though still attached to the [ecclesiastical province of Rouen](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province_of_Rouen "Ecclesiastical province of Rouen"). In the 1259 [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1259) "Treaty of Paris (1259)"), [Henry III of England](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") recognized the legitimacy of the French possession of mainland Normandy. His successors, however, often fought to regain control of their ancient fiefdom.

The *Charte aux Normands* granted by [Louis X of France](/wiki/Louis_X_of_France "Louis X of France") in 1315 (and later re-confirmed in 1339) – like the analogous [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") granted in England in the aftermath of 1204 – guaranteed the liberties and privileges of the [province of Normandy](/wiki/Province_of_Normandy "Province of Normandy").

Normandy was devastated by various civil wars and the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"). Between 1419 and 1450, the English controlled all of Normandy apart from [Mont-Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont-Saint-Michel "Mont-Saint-Michel"), and made Rouen the seat of their power in France. Normandy ultimately saw its population decline by three quarters as a result of the various conflicts which took place in the region during the late Middle Ages.[[29]](#cite_note-32) Afterwards, prosperity returned to Normandy until the [Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"). When many Norman towns ([Alençon](/wiki/Alen%C3%A7on "Alençon"), Rouen, [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen"), [Coutances](/wiki/Coutances "Coutances"), [Bayeux](/wiki/Bayeux "Bayeux")) joined the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), battles ensued throughout the province. In the Channel Islands, a period of [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") following the Reformation was suppressed when [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") was imposed following the [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration").

[Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") left the port of [Honfleur](/wiki/Honfleur "Honfleur") in 1604 and founded [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"). Four years later, he founded the [City of Québec](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"). From then onwards, Normans engaged in a policy of expansion in North America. They continued the exploration of the New World: [René-Robert Cavelier de La Salle](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Robert_Cavelier_de_La_Salle "René-Robert Cavelier de La Salle") travelled in the area of the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), then on the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"). [Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville](/wiki/Pierre_Le_Moyne_d%27Iberville "Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville") and his brother [Lemoyne de Bienville](/wiki/Lemoyne_de_Bienville "Lemoyne de Bienville") founded [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), [Biloxi](/wiki/Biloxi,_Mississippi "Biloxi, Mississippi"), Mobile and New Orleans. Territories located between Québec and the [Mississippi Delta](/wiki/Mississippi_Delta "Mississippi Delta") were opened up to establish [Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") and [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)"). Colonists from Normandy were among the most active in [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), comprising [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"), Canada, and Louisiana.

Honfleur and [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre") were two of the principal [slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade") ports of France.

### 18th century to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 18th century to present")]

Although agriculture remained important, industries such as weaving, metallurgy, sugar refining, ceramics, and shipbuilding were introduced and developed.

In the 1780s, the economic crisis and the crisis of the *[Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime")* struck Normandy as well as other parts of the nation, leading to the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). Bad harvests, technical progress and the effects of the [Eden Agreement](/wiki/Eden_Agreement "Eden Agreement") signed in 1786 affected employment and the economy of the province. Normans laboured under a heavy fiscal burden.

In 1790, the five departments of Normandy replaced the former province.

On 13 July 1793, the Norman [Charlotte Corday](/wiki/Charlotte_Corday "Charlotte Corday") assassinated [Jean-Paul Marat](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Marat "Jean-Paul Marat").

The Normans reacted little to the many political upheavals which characterized the 19th century. Overall, they warily accepted the changes of régime ([First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire"), [Bourbon Restoration](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France"), [July Monarchy](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy"), [French Second Republic](/wiki/French_Second_Republic "French Second Republic"), [Second French Empire](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire"), [French Third Republic](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic")).

Following the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") and the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") (1792–1815), there was an economic revival that included the mechanization of textile manufacturing and the introduction of the first trains.

Also, with seaside tourism in the 19th century came the advent of the first beach resorts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/NormandySupply_edit.jpg/500px-NormandySupply_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:NormandySupply_edit.jpg)

[Omaha Beach](/wiki/Omaha_Beach "Omaha Beach") during the Allied invasion of Normandy, mid-June 1944

During the Second World War, following the [armistice of 22 June 1940](/wiki/Second_Armistice_at_Compi%C3%A8gne "Second Armistice at Compiègne"), continental Normandy was part of the [German occupied zone of France](/wiki/Military_Administration_in_France_(Nazi_Germany) "Military Administration in France (Nazi Germany)"). The Channel Islands were [occupied by German forces](/wiki/German_occupation_of_the_Channel_Islands "German occupation of the Channel Islands") between 30 June 1940 and 9 May 1945. The town of [Dieppe](/wiki/Dieppe "Dieppe") was the site of the unsuccessful [Dieppe Raid](/wiki/Dieppe_Raid "Dieppe Raid") by [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") forces.

The Allies coordinated a massive build-up of troops and supplies to support a large-scale invasion of Normandy in the [D-Day landings](/wiki/D-Day_landings "D-Day landings") on 6 June 1944 under the code name [Operation Overlord](/wiki/Operation_Overlord "Operation Overlord"). German forces dug into fortified emplacements above the beaches. [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen"), [Cherbourg](/wiki/Cherbourg "Cherbourg"), [Carentan](/wiki/Carentan "Carentan"), [Falaise](/wiki/Falaise,_Calvados "Falaise, Calvados") and other Norman towns endured many casualties in the [Battle of Normandy](/wiki/Invasion_of_Normandy "Invasion of Normandy"), which continued until the closing of the so-called [Falaise gap](/wiki/Falaise_pocket "Falaise pocket") between [Chambois](/wiki/Chambois,_Orne "Chambois, Orne") and [Mont Ormel](/wiki/Mont_Ormel "Mont Ormel"). The liberation of [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre") followed. This was a significant turning point in the war in western Europe and led to the restoration of the French Republic.

The remainder of Normandy was liberated by Allied forces only on 9 May 1945 at the end of the war, when the [Channel Island occupation](/wiki/Liberation_of_the_German-occupied_Channel_Islands "Liberation of the German-occupied Channel Islands") effectively ended.

Despite the renunciation of the Duke of Normandy title by [Henry III of England](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") in the 1259 [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1259) "Treaty of Paris (1259)"),[[30]](#cite_note-33) and the extinction of the duchy itself in modern-day, republican France, in the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") the [monarch of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") (whether a king or queen) is regardless still sometimes informally referred to by the title "Duke of Normandy".

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Geography")]

The island of [Mont-Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont-Saint-Michel "Mont-Saint-Michel"), the most visited monument in Normandy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Falaises_Etretat_2012.jpg/250px-Falaises_Etretat_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Falaises_Etretat_2012.jpg)

The *Arche* and the *Aiguille* of the cliffs of [Étretat](/wiki/%C3%89tretat "Étretat")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Jonquerets-de-Livret-_batiment_du_Pressoir.JPG/250px-Jonquerets-de-Livret-_batiment_du_Pressoir.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jonquerets-de-Livret-_batiment_du_Pressoir.JPG)

A typical Norman [thatched](/wiki/Thatching "Thatching") building. This is now a village hall.

The historical Duchy of Normandy was a formerly independent duchy occupying the lower Seine area, the [Pays de Caux](/wiki/Pays_de_Caux "Pays de Caux") and the region to the west through the [Pays d'Auge](/wiki/Pays_d%27Auge "Pays d'Auge") as far as the [Cotentin Peninsula](/wiki/Cotentin_Peninsula "Cotentin Peninsula") and Channel Islands.

Western Normandy belongs to the [Armorican Massif](/wiki/Armorican_Massif "Armorican Massif"), while most of the region lies in the [Paris Basin](/wiki/Paris_Basin "Paris Basin"). France's oldest rocks are exposed in Jobourg, on the [Cotentin](/wiki/Cotentin "Cotentin") peninsula.[[31]](#cite_note-34) The region is bounded to the north and west by the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). There are granite cliffs in the west and limestone cliffs in the east. There are also long stretches of beach in the centre of the region. The *[bocage](/wiki/Bocage "Bocage")* typical of the western areas caused problems for the invading forces in the [Battle of Normandy](/wiki/Invasion_of_Normandy "Invasion of Normandy"). A notable feature of the landscape is created by the [meanders](/wiki/Meander "Meander") of the Seine as it approaches its estuary.

The highest point is the Signal d'Écouves (417 m), in the Armorican Massif.

Normandy is sparsely forested:[[32]](#cite_note-35) 12.8% of the territory is wooded, compared to a French average of 23.6%, although the proportion varies between the departments. Eure has the most cover, at 21%, while Manche has the least, at 4%, a characteristic shared with the Channel Islands.

The [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") are considered culturally and historically a part of Normandy. However, they are British [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"), and are not part of the modern French [administrative region of Normandy](/wiki/Normandy_(administrative_region) "Normandy (administrative region)").

Although the British abandoned claims to mainland Normandy and other French possessions in 1801, the monarch of the United Kingdom retains the title Duke of Normandy in respect to the Channel Islands.[[33]](#cite_note-36) The Channel Islands (except for [Chausey](/wiki/Chausey "Chausey")) remain [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") of the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") in the present era. Thus the [Loyal Toast](/wiki/Loyal_Toast "Loyal Toast") in the Channel Islands is *Le Roi, notre Duc* ("The King, our Duke"). The British monarch is understood to *not* be the Duke with regards to mainland Normandy described herein, by virtue of the [Treaty of Paris of 1259](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1259) "Treaty of Paris (1259)"), the abandonment of claims to France in 1801, and the belief that the rights of succession to that title are subject to [Salic Law](/wiki/Salic_Law "Salic Law") which excludes inheritance through female heirs.[[34]](#cite_note-37)

### Mainland Normandy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Mainland Normandy")]

The modern region of [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy_(administrative_region) "Normandy (administrative region)") was created by the territorial reform of French Regions in 2014 by the merger of [Lower Normandy](/wiki/Lower_Normandy "Lower Normandy"), and [Upper Normandy](/wiki/Upper_Normandy "Upper Normandy"). The new region took effect on 1 January 2016, after the [regional elections in December 2015](/wiki/2015_French_regional_elections "2015 French regional elections").[[35]](#cite_note-38)

The [Regional Council](/wiki/Regional_Council_(France) "Regional Council (France)") has 102 members who are elected under a system of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"). The executive consists of a president and vice-presidents. [Hervé Morin](/wiki/Herv%C3%A9_Morin "Hervé Morin") from the Centre party was elected president of the council in January 2016.

### Channel Islands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Channel Islands")]

The Channel Islands are not part of French territory, but are instead British [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"). They are self-governing, each having its own parliament, government and legal system. The head of state of both territories is [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") and each have an appointed [Lieutenant-Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor "Lieutenant governor").

The [Bailiwick of Guernsey](/wiki/Bailiwick_of_Guernsey "Bailiwick of Guernsey") comprises three separate jurisdictions: [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey"), [Alderney](/wiki/Alderney "Alderney") and [Sark](/wiki/Sark "Sark"). Administratively, Herm forms part of [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey").

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Economy")]

Much of Normandy is predominantly agricultural in character, with cattle breeding the most important sector (although in decline from the peak levels of the 1970s and 1980s). The *[bocage](/wiki/Bocage "Bocage")* is a patchwork of small fields with high hedges, typical of western areas. Areas near the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") (the former [Upper Normandy](/wiki/Upper_Normandy "Upper Normandy") region) contain a higher concentration of industry. Normandy is a significant [cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider")-producing region, and also produces [calvados](/wiki/Calvados_(spirit) "Calvados (spirit)"), a distilled cider or [apple brandy](/wiki/Apple_brandy "Apple brandy"). Other activities of economic importance are dairy produce, [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax") (60% of production in France), [horse breeding](/wiki/Horse_breeding "Horse breeding") (including two French national stud farms), fishing, seafood, and tourism. The region contains three French [nuclear power stations](/wiki/Nuclear_power_station "Nuclear power station").
There is also easy access to and from the UK using the ports of [Cherbourg](/wiki/Cherbourg "Cherbourg"), [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen") ([Ouistreham](/wiki/Ouistreham "Ouistreham")), [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre") and [Dieppe](/wiki/Dieppe,_Seine-Maritime "Dieppe, Seine-Maritime").[[36]](#cite_note-39) Jersey and Guernsey are often considered to be tax havens, due to having large financial services sectors and low tax rates.[[37]](#cite_note-40)

In January 2006 the population of French Normandy (including the part of [Perche](/wiki/Perche "Perche") which lies inside the [Orne](/wiki/Orne "Orne") *[département](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France")* but excluding the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands")) was estimated at 3,260,000 with an average population density of 109 inhabitants per km2, just under the French national average, but rising to 147 for [Upper Normandy](/wiki/Upper_Normandy "Upper Normandy"). ## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Flag")]

The traditional provincial [flag of Normandy](/wiki/Flag_of_Normandy "Flag of Normandy"), *gules, two leopards passant or*, is used in the region and its predecessors. The three-leopard version (known in the Norman language as *les treis cats*, "the three cats") is used by some associations and individuals, especially those who support cultural links with the Channel Islands and England. Jersey and Guernsey use three leopards in their national symbols. The leopards represents the strength and courage Normandy has towards the neighbouring provinces.

The unofficial anthem of the region is the song "[Ma Normandie](/wiki/Ma_Normandie "Ma Normandie")".

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Language")]

Main articles: [Norman language](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language") and [Norman toponymy](/wiki/Norman_toponymy "Norman toponymy")

The Norman language, including its insular variations [Jèrriais](/wiki/J%C3%A8rriais "Jèrriais") and [Guernésiais](/wiki/Guern%C3%A9siais "Guernésiais"), is a [regional language](/wiki/Languages_of_France "Languages of France"), spoken by a minority of the population on the continent and the islands, with a concentration in the Cotentin Peninsula in the far west (the [Cotentinais](/wiki/Cotentinais "Cotentinais") dialect), and in the [Pays de Caux](/wiki/Pays_de_Caux "Pays de Caux") in the East (the [Cauchois dialect](/wiki/Cauchois_dialect "Cauchois dialect")).

Many words and place names demonstrate the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") and [Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse_language "Old Norse language") ([Anglo-Scandinavian](/wiki/Anglo-Scandinavian "Anglo-Scandinavian")) influence in this [Oïl language](/wiki/O%C3%AFl_languages "Oïl languages"); for example, words : *mauve* (seagull), *fifotte* (starfish), *hâ* (catshark), *mucre* (humid, wet), *(é)griller* (slide, slip), *fale* (throat), etc. place-names : *-bec* (stream), *-fleur* (river), *[-hou](/wiki/-hou "-hou")* (island), *-tot* (homestead), *-dal* / *-dalle* (valley), *Hogue* / *Hougue* (hill, mound), *-lon* / *-londe* (grove, wood), *-vy* / *-vic* (bay, cove), *-mare* (pond), *-beuf* (booth, cabin), etc.[[43]](#cite_note-46) French is the only [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") in continental Normandy and English is also an official language in the Channel Islands.

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Main article: [Architecture of Normandy](/wiki/Architecture_of_Normandy "Architecture of Normandy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Dauville_Hotel_Normandie.jpg/250px-Dauville_Hotel_Normandie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dauville_Hotel_Normandie.jpg)

A Norman style construction in [Deauville](/wiki/Deauville "Deauville")

Architecturally, Norman cathedrals, abbeys (such as the [Abbey of Bec](/wiki/Abbey_of_Bec "Abbey of Bec")) and castles characterise the former duchy in a way that mirrors the similar pattern of [Norman architecture](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") in England following the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") of 1066.

Domestic architecture in upper Normandy is typified by [half-timbered](/wiki/Half-timbered_construction "Half-timbered construction") buildings that also recall vernacular English architecture, although the farm enclosures of the more harshly landscaped Pays de Caux are a more idiosyncratic response to socio-economic and climatic imperatives. Much urban architectural heritage was destroyed during the Battle of Normandy in 1944 – post-war urban reconstruction, such as in Le Havre and Saint-Lô, could be said to demonstrate both the virtues and vices of [modernist](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") and [brutalist](/wiki/Brutalism "Brutalism") trends of the 1950s and 1960s. Le Havre, the city rebuilt by [Auguste Perret](/wiki/Auguste_Perret "Auguste Perret"), was added to Unesco's World Heritage List in 2005.

[Vernacular architecture](/wiki/Vernacular_architecture "Vernacular architecture") in lower Normandy takes its form from [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite"), the predominant local building material. The Channel Islands also share this influence – [Chausey](/wiki/Chausey "Chausey") was for many years a source of quarried granite, including that used for the construction of [Mont Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont_Saint-Michel "Mont Saint-Michel").

The south part of [Bagnoles-de-l'Orne](/wiki/Bagnoles-de-l%27Orne "Bagnoles-de-l'Orne") is filled with bourgeois villas in *[Belle Époque](/wiki/Belle_%C3%89poque "Belle Époque")* style with polychrome façades, bow windows and unique roofing. This area, built between 1886 and 1914, has an authentic "Bagnolese" style and is typical of high-society country vacation of the time. The Chapel of Saint Germanus (*Chapelle Saint-Germain*) at [Querqueville](/wiki/Querqueville "Querqueville") with its [trefoil](/wiki/Trefoil "Trefoil") floorplan incorporates elements of one of the earliest surviving places of Christian worship in the Cotentin – perhaps second only to the Gallo-Roman baptistry at [Port-Bail](/wiki/Port-Bail "Port-Bail"). It is dedicated to [Germanus of Normandy](/wiki/Germanus_of_Normandy "Germanus of Normandy").

### Gastronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Gastronomy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Vachesnormandes.jpg/250px-Vachesnormandes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vachesnormandes.jpg)

[Normande cow](/wiki/Normande_Cattle "Normande Cattle")

Parts of Normandy consist of rolling countryside typified by pasture for dairy cattle and apple orchards. A wide range of dairy products are produced and exported. Norman cheeses include [Camembert](/wiki/Camembert_(cheese) "Camembert (cheese)"), [Livarot](/wiki/Livarot_cheese "Livarot cheese"), [Pont l'Évêque](/wiki/Pont-l%27%C3%89v%C3%AAque_cheese "Pont-l'Évêque cheese"), [Brillat-Savarin](/wiki/Brillat-Savarin_cheese "Brillat-Savarin cheese"), [Neufchâtel](/wiki/Neufch%C3%A2tel_(cheese) "Neufchâtel (cheese)"), [Petit Suisse](/wiki/Petit_suisse_(cheese) "Petit suisse (cheese)") and [Boursin](/wiki/Boursin_(cheese) "Boursin (cheese)").[[44]](#cite_note-47) Normandy butter and Normandy cream are lavishly used in gastronomic specialties. [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") and [Guernsey cattle](/wiki/Guernsey_cattle "Guernsey cattle") are famous cattle breeds worldwide, especially to North America.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Reflets_de_France_Cidre.jpg/250px-Reflets_de_France_Cidre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reflets_de_France_Cidre.jpg)

[Cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider") from Normandy

Turbot and oysters from the Cotentin Peninsula are major delicacies throughout France. Normandy is the chief [oyster](/wiki/Oyster "Oyster")-cultivating, scallop-exporting, and mussel-raising region in France.

Normandy is a major [cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider")-producing region (very little wine is produced). [Perry](/wiki/Perry "Perry") is also produced, but in less significant quantities. Apple brandy, of which the most famous variety is [calvados](/wiki/Calvados_(spirit) "Calvados (spirit)"), is also popular. The mealtime *trou normand*, or "Norman hole", is a pause between meal courses in which diners partake of a glassful of calvados in order to improve the appetite and make room for the next course, and this is still observed in many homes and restaurants. *[Pommeau](/wiki/Pommeau "Pommeau")* is an *[apéritif](/wiki/Ap%C3%A9ritif "Apéritif")* produced by blending unfermented cider and apple brandy. Another aperitif is the *[kir](/wiki/Kir_(cocktail) "Kir (cocktail)") normand*, a measure of [crème de cassis](/wiki/Cr%C3%A8me_de_cassis "Crème de cassis") topped up with cider. *[Bénédictine](/wiki/B%C3%A9n%C3%A9dictine "Bénédictine")* is produced in [Fécamp](/wiki/F%C3%A9camp "Fécamp").

Other regional specialities include *[tripes à la mode de Caen](/wiki/Tripes_%C3%A0_la_mode_de_Caen "Tripes à la mode de Caen")*, *[andouilles](/wiki/Andouille "Andouille")* and *andouillettes*, [salade cauchoise](/wiki/Salade_cauchoise "Salade cauchoise"), salt meadow (*pré salé*) lamb, seafood (mussels, [scallops](/wiki/Scallop "Scallop"), lobsters, mackerel...), and *[teurgoule](/wiki/Teurgoule "Teurgoule")* (spiced rice pudding).

Normandy dishes include duckling *à la rouennaise*, sautéed chicken *yvetois*, and goose *en daube*. Rabbit is cooked with [morels](/wiki/Morel "Morel"), or *à la havraise* (stuffed with truffled pigs' trotters). Other dishes are sheep's trotters *à la rouennaise*, casseroled veal, larded calf's liver braised with carrots, and veal (or turkey) in cream and mushrooms.

Normandy is also noted for its pastries. Normandy turns out *douillons* (pears baked in pastry), *craquelins*, *roulettes* in Rouen, *fouaces* in Caen, *fallues* in [Lisieux](/wiki/Lisieux "Lisieux"), *sablés* in Lisieux. It is the birthplace of [brioches](/wiki/Brioche "Brioche") (especially those from [Évreux](/wiki/%C3%89vreux "Évreux") and [Gisors](/wiki/Gisors "Gisors")). Confectionery of the region includes Rouen apple sugar, Isigny caramels, [Bayeux](/wiki/Bayeux "Bayeux") mint chews, Falaise berlingots, [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre") marzipans, [Argentan](/wiki/Argentan "Argentan") *croquettes*, and Rouen [macaroons](/wiki/Macaroons "Macaroons").

Normandy is the native land of [Taillevent](/wiki/Taillevent "Taillevent"), cook of the kings of France [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V_of_France "Charles V of France") and [Charles VI](/wiki/Charles_VI_of_France "Charles VI of France"). He wrote the earliest French cookery book named *Le Viandier*. *[Confiture de lait](/wiki/Confiture_de_lait "Confiture de lait")* was also made in Normandy around the 14th century.

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Literature")]

See also: [Anglo-Norman literature](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_literature "Anglo-Norman literature") and [Gesta Normannorum Ducum](/wiki/Gesta_Normannorum_Ducum "Gesta Normannorum Ducum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Wace_illustration_Roman_de_Rou_1824.jpg/250px-Wace_illustration_Roman_de_Rou_1824.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wace_illustration_Roman_de_Rou_1824.jpg)

[Wace](/wiki/Wace "Wace") presents his *[Roman de Rou](/wiki/Roman_de_Rou "Roman de Rou")* to Henry II, illustration 1824.

The dukes of Normandy commissioned and inspired epic literature to record and legitimise their rule. [Wace](/wiki/Wace "Wace"), [Orderic Vitalis](/wiki/Orderic_Vitalis "Orderic Vitalis") and [Stephen of Rouen](/wiki/Stephen_of_Rouen "Stephen of Rouen") were among those who wrote in the service of the dukes. After the division of 1204, French literature provided the model for the development of literature in Normandy. [Olivier Basselin](/wiki/Olivier_Basselin "Olivier Basselin") wrote of the Vaux de Vire, the origin of literary *[vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville")*. Notable Norman writers include [Jean Marot](/wiki/Jean_Marot "Jean Marot"), [Rémy Belleau](/wiki/R%C3%A9my_Belleau "Rémy Belleau"), [Guy de Maupassant](/wiki/Guy_de_Maupassant "Guy de Maupassant"), [Jules Barbey d'Aurevilly](/wiki/Jules_Barbey_d%27Aurevilly "Jules Barbey d'Aurevilly"), [Gustave Flaubert](/wiki/Gustave_Flaubert "Gustave Flaubert"), [Octave Mirbeau](/wiki/Octave_Mirbeau "Octave Mirbeau"), and [Remy de Gourmont](/wiki/Remy_de_Gourmont "Remy de Gourmont"), and [Alexis de Tocqueville](/wiki/Alexis_de_Tocqueville "Alexis de Tocqueville"). The Corneille brothers, [Pierre](/wiki/Pierre_Corneille "Pierre Corneille") and [Thomas](/wiki/Thomas_Corneille "Thomas Corneille"), born in Rouen, were great figures of French classical literature.

David Ferrand (1591–1660) in his *Muse Normande* established a landmark of Norman language literature. In the 16th and 17th centuries, the workers and merchants of Rouen established a tradition of polemical and satirical literature in a form of language called the *parler purin*. At the end of the 18th century and beginning of the 19th century, a new movement arose in the Channel Islands, led by writers such as [George Métivier](/wiki/George_M%C3%A9tivier "George Métivier"), which sparked a literary renaissance on the Norman mainland. In exile in Jersey and then Guernsey, [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo") took an interest in the vernacular literature. *[Les Travailleurs de la mer](/wiki/Les_Travailleurs_de_la_mer "Les Travailleurs de la mer")* is a well-known novel by Hugo set in the Channel Islands. The boom in insular literature in the early 19th century encouraged production especially in La Hague and around Cherbourg, where Alfred Rossel, Louis Beuve and [Côtis-Capel](/wiki/C%C3%B4tis-Capel "Côtis-Capel") became active. The typical medium for literary expression in Norman has traditionally been newspaper columns and almanacs. The novel *Zabeth* by André Louis which appeared in 1969 was the first novel published in Norman.

### Painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Painting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Claude_Monet_-_Woman_with_a_Parasol_-_Madame_Monet_and_Her_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Claude_Monet_-_Woman_with_a_Parasol_-_Madame_Monet_and_Her_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Claude_Monet_-_Woman_with_a_Parasol_-_Madame_Monet_and_Her_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

[Claude Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet"), *[Woman with a Parasol – Madame Monet and Her Son](/wiki/Woman_with_a_Parasol_%E2%80%93_Madame_Monet_and_Her_Son "Woman with a Parasol – Madame Monet and Her Son")*, 1875

Normandy has a rich tradition of painting and gave to France some of its most important artists.

In the 17th century, some major French painters were Normans like [Nicolas Poussin](/wiki/Nicolas_Poussin "Nicolas Poussin"), born in [Les Andelys](/wiki/Les_Andelys "Les Andelys") and [Jean Jouvenet](/wiki/Jean_Jouvenet "Jean Jouvenet").

[Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") drew painters to the Channel coasts of Normandy. [Richard Parkes Bonington](/wiki/Richard_Parkes_Bonington "Richard Parkes Bonington") and [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner") crossed the Channel from Great Britain, attracted by the light and landscapes. [Théodore Géricault](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odore_G%C3%A9ricault "Théodore Géricault"), a native of Rouen, was a notable figure in the Romantic movement, its famous *[Radeau de la Méduse](/wiki/Le_Radeau_de_la_M%C3%A9duse "Le Radeau de la Méduse")* being considered come the breakthrough of pictorial romanticism in France when it was officially presented at the [Salon of 1819](/wiki/Salon_of_1819 "Salon of 1819"). The competing Realist tendency was represented by [Jean-François Millet](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Millet "Jean-François Millet"), a native of La Hague. The landscape painter [Eugène Boudin](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Boudin "Eugène Boudin"), born in Honfleur, was a determining influence on the impressionists and was highly considered by Monet.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Eug%C3%A8ne_Chigot_%28French%29%2C_Printemps_en_Normandie_%28Springtime_in_Normandy%29.jpg/250px-Eug%C3%A8ne_Chigot_%28French%29%2C_Printemps_en_Normandie_%28Springtime_in_Normandy%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eug%C3%A8ne_Chigot_(French),_Printemps_en_Normandie_(Springtime_in_Normandy).jpg)

[Eugène Chigot](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Chigot "Eugène Chigot") (1860–1923), Printemps en Normandie (Springtime in Normandy) (1914/15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Robert_Antoine_Pinchon%2C_Un_apr%C3%A8s-midi_%C3%A0_l%27Ile_aux_Cerises%2C_Rouen%2C_oil_on_canvas%2C_50_x_61.2_cm.jpg/250px-Robert_Antoine_Pinchon%2C_Un_apr%C3%A8s-midi_%C3%A0_l%27Ile_aux_Cerises%2C_Rouen%2C_oil_on_canvas%2C_50_x_61.2_cm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Antoine_Pinchon,_Un_apr%C3%A8s-midi_%C3%A0_l%27Ile_aux_Cerises,_Rouen,_oil_on_canvas,_50_x_61.2_cm.jpg)

[Robert Antoine Pinchon](/wiki/Robert_Antoine_Pinchon "Robert Antoine Pinchon"), *Un après-midi à l'Ile aux Cerises, Rouen*, oil on canvas, 50 x 61.2 cm

Breaking away from the more formalised and classical themes of the early part of the 19th century, Impressionist painters preferred to paint outdoors, in natural light, and to concentrate on landscapes, towns and scenes of daily life.

Leader of the movement and father of modern painting, [Claude Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet") is one of the best known Impressionists and a major character in Normandy's artistic heritage. His [house and gardens](/wiki/Fondation_Monet_in_Giverny "Fondation Monet in Giverny") at [Giverny](/wiki/Giverny "Giverny") are one of the region's major tourist sites, much visited for their beauty and their water lilies, as well as for their importance to Monet's artistic inspiration. Normandy was at the heart of his creation, from the paintings of Rouen's cathedral to the famous depictions of the cliffs at [Étretat](/wiki/%C3%89tretat "Étretat"), the beach and port at [Fécamp](/wiki/F%C3%A9camp "Fécamp") and the sunrise at [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre"). It was *[Impression, Sunrise](/wiki/Impression,_Sunrise "Impression, Sunrise")*, Monet's painting of Le Havre, that led to the movement being dubbed [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"). After Monet, all the main [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") painters of the 1870s and 1880s came to Normandy to paint its landscapes and its changing lights, concentrating along the Seine valley and the Norman coast.

Landscapes and scenes of daily life were also immortalised on canvas by artists that have included : [William Turner](/wiki/William_Turner_(artist) "William Turner (artist)"), [Gustave Courbet](/wiki/Gustave_Courbet "Gustave Courbet"), the Honfleur born Eugène Boudin, [Camille Pissarro](/wiki/Camille_Pissarro "Camille Pissarro"), [Alfred Sisley](/wiki/Alfred_Sisley "Alfred Sisley"), [Auguste Renoir](/wiki/Auguste_Renoir "Auguste Renoir"), [Gustave Caillebotte](/wiki/Gustave_Caillebotte "Gustave Caillebotte"), [Eugène Chigot](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Chigot "Eugène Chigot"), [Paul Gauguin](/wiki/Paul_Gauguin "Paul Gauguin"), [Georges Seurat](/wiki/Georges_Seurat "Georges Seurat"), [Paul Signac](/wiki/Paul_Signac "Paul Signac"), [Pierre Bonnard](/wiki/Pierre_Bonnard "Pierre Bonnard"), [Georges Braque](/wiki/Georges_Braque "Georges Braque") and [Pablo Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso"). While Monet's work adorns galleries and collections all over the world, a remarkable quantity of Impressionist works can be found in galleries throughout Normandy, such as the [Museum of Fine Arts](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_des_Beaux-Arts_de_Rouen "Musée des Beaux-Arts de Rouen") in Rouen, the Musée Eugène Boudin in [Honfleur](/wiki/Honfleur "Honfleur") or the [André Malraux Museum](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_Malraux "Musée Malraux") in [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre").

[Maurice Denis](/wiki/Maurice_Denis "Maurice Denis"), one of the leaders and theoricists of the [Nabis](/wiki/Les_Nabis "Les Nabis") movement in the 1890s, was a native of Granville, in the department of Manche. [Marie-Thérèse Auffray](/wiki/Marie-Th%C3%A9r%C3%A8se_Auffray "Marie-Thérèse Auffray"), an [expressionist](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism") painter and member of the French resistance during WWII, lived and painted in the village of [Échauffour](/wiki/%C3%89chauffour "Échauffour").

The *[Société Normande de Peinture Moderne](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_Normande_de_Peinture_Moderne "Société Normande de Peinture Moderne")* was founded in 1909 by [Pierre Dumont](/wiki/Pierre_Dumont_(painter) "Pierre Dumont (painter)"), [Robert Antoine Pinchon](/wiki/Robert_Antoine_Pinchon "Robert Antoine Pinchon"), Yvonne Barbier and Eugène Tirvert. Among members were [Raoul Dufy](/wiki/Raoul_Dufy "Raoul Dufy"), a native of Le Havre, [Albert Marquet](/wiki/Albert_Marquet "Albert Marquet"), [Francis Picabia](/wiki/Francis_Picabia "Francis Picabia") and [Maurice Utrillo](/wiki/Maurice_Utrillo "Maurice Utrillo"). Also in this movement were the Duchamp brothers, [Jacques Villon](/wiki/Jacques_Villon "Jacques Villon") and [Marcel Duchamp](/wiki/Marcel_Duchamp "Marcel Duchamp"), considered one of the father of modern art, also natives of Normandy. [Jean Dubuffet](/wiki/Jean_Dubuffet "Jean Dubuffet"), one of the leading French artist of the 1940s and the 1950s was born in Le Havre.

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normandy&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Rouen_Cathedral_as_seen_from_Gros_Horloge_140215_4.jpg/250px-Rouen_Cathedral_as_seen_from_Gros_Horloge_140215_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rouen_Cathedral_as_seen_from_Gros_Horloge_140215_4.jpg)

[Rouen Cathedral](/wiki/Rouen_Cathedral "Rouen Cathedral")

Christian missionaries implanted [monastic communities](/wiki/Monastic_communities "Monastic communities") in the territory in the 5th and 6th centuries. Some of these missionaries came from across the [Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). The influence of [Celtic Christianity](/wiki/Celtic_Christianity "Celtic Christianity") can still be found in the [Cotentin](/wiki/Cotentin "Cotentin"). By the terms of the treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte, Rollo, a Viking pagan, accepted Christianity and was baptised. The Duchy of Normandy was therefore formally a Christian state from its foundation. The cathedrals of Normandy have exerted influence down the centuries in matters of both faith and politics. [King Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England") of England did [penance](/wiki/Penance "Penance") at the cathedral of [Avranches](/wiki/Avranches_Cathedral "Avranches Cathedral") on 21 May 1172 and was absolved from the censures incurred by the assassination of [Thomas Becket](/wiki/Thomas_Becket "Thomas Becket"). [Mont Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont_Saint-Michel "Mont Saint-Michel") is a historic [pilgrimage](/wiki/Pilgrimage "Pilgrimage") site.

Normandy does not have one generally agreed [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint"), although this title has been ascribed to Saint [Michael](/wiki/Michael_(archangel) "Michael (archangel)"), and to [Saint Ouen](/wiki/Saint_Ouen "Saint Ouen"). Many saints have been revered in Normandy down the centuries, including:

* [Aubert](/wiki/St._Aubert "St. Aubert") who's remembered as the founder of [Mont Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont_Saint-Michel "Mont Saint-Michel")
* [Marcouf](/wiki/Saint_Marcouf "Saint Marcouf") and [Laud](/wiki/Laud_of_Coutances "Laud of Coutances") who are important saints in Normandy
* [Helier](/wiki/Helier "Helier") and [Samson of Dol](/wiki/Samson_of_Dol "Samson of Dol") who are evangelizers of the Channel Islands
* [Thomas Becket](/wiki/Thomas_Becket "Thomas Becket"), an [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Normans "Anglo-Normans") whose parents were from Rouen, who was the object of a considerable following in mainland Normandy following his martyrdom
* [Joan of Arc](/wiki/Joan_of_Arc "Joan of Arc") who was [martyred](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") in Rouen, and who is especially remembered in that city
* [Thérèse de Lisieux](/wiki/Th%C3%A9r%C3%A8se_de_Lisieux "Thérèse de Lisieux") whose birthplace in [Alençon](/wiki/Alen%C3%A7on "Alençon") and later home in Lisieux are a focus for religious pilgrims.
* [Germanus of Normandy](/wiki/Germanus_of_Normandy "Germanus of Normandy")

Since the [1905 French law on the Separation of the Churches and the State](/wiki/1905_French_law_on_the_Separation_of_the_Churches_and_the_State "1905 French law on the Separation of the Churches and the State"), there is no established church in mainland Normandy. In the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands"), the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") is the [established church](/wiki/Established_church "Established church").
